pattern: A circular row forbids robbing both ends together, so split into two linear sub-rows — one excluding the first house, one excluding the last — solve each with the classic take-or-skip recurrence, and take the better of the two.
hints: The only complication versus a straight row is that the first and last houses are now adjacent, so they can never both be robbed.
If you simply forbid the first house, or alternatively forbid the last house, each case is an ordinary non-circular robbery problem.
Run the linear solver on nums[1:] and on nums[:-1] and return the maximum; handle the single-house case directly since both slices would be empty.

edge_inputs.description: three houses in a circle, ends cannot both be taken
non-adjacent picks beat the circular ends
three equal-ish houses, middle alone wins
two houses, take the larger one only
large ends tempt taking both but circle forbids it

stress.description: random circular row kept short so the subset-enumeration oracle stays fast
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(16, size)))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 1000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
stress.note: length capped at 16 because the brute oracle enumerates all subsets (2^n)
